topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office hummed with a dull, monotonous sound, a soundtrack to my slow descent. I was meticulously filing quarterly reports, the crisp paper a stark contrast to the murky reality settling in my stomach. Sarah, the VP of Operations, was pacing by the window, her usual energetic stride replaced by a tight, jerky motion. She kept glancing at the mahogany conference table, not at the documents, but at the empty space where the new signage was supposed to go. I just kept my hands moving, the clicks of the stapler sounding deafeningly loud in the silence of my own mind. It wasn't a shock; it was just… expected. Like watching the tide come in, knowing the shore will eventually change shape. I finished the last file, slid it into the designated box, and stared at the beige carpet. Another day, another layer peeled away, leaving only the bare structure underneath.

[story 2]

I traced the grain of the wooden desk with my fingertip, the cool surface offering no comfort. The air in the breakroom felt thin, stale, as if the oxygen itself had been rationed. Mark leaned against the counter, nursing a lukewarm coffee, and his voice was low, devoid of inflection. "Did you see the memo?" he asked, not looking up from his screen. I nodded once, a small, almost involuntary movement. My shoulders dropped an inch, a subtle surrender of tension I hadn't realized I was holding. It was all just noise now—the incessant ringing phones, the distant murmur of colleagues talking about weekend plans. I took a slow sip of water, the coolness doing little to quell the heavy feeling in my chest. The future felt like a vast, unnavigable ocean, and I was simply waiting for the current to pull me along.

[story 3]

The scent of fresh paint, a sharp, unfamiliar aroma, permeated the hallway. I stood by the water cooler, pretending to examine the condensation on the plastic bottle, while my eyes tracked the movement of the executive team. They weren't laughing; they were calculating. I watched Mr. Henderson adjust his tie, a gesture that seemed overly deliberate, like someone checking the seams of a carefully constructed costume. My thoughts were sluggish, like thick syrup. I remembered the promises made years ago, the ambitious projections, and now, this quiet dismantling. I didn't move to confront anyone. Instead, I pulled my cardigan tighter around myself, a small, futile attempt to hold onto some semblance of personal space in a world that was rapidly rearranging itself around a new, unknown center. It was time to just observe the inevitable conclusion.
